title: 2026 Arkansas Supreme Court election
description: This race is for a nonpartisan seat on the Arkansas Supreme Court, the state’s highest court, in the March 3, 2026 election. It drew unusual attention because state law barred appointed justices from running for re-election to the same seat, so two incumbent appointees ended up running for each other’s seats. The court matters because it decides major questions of state constitutional law, election rules, criminal and civil appeals, and other high-stakes disputes affecting Arkansas government. The main campaign contrast centered on judicial philosophy: Nicholas Bronni emphasized originalism and a more conservative approach, while John Adams stressed judicial restraint, independence, and keeping the court nonpartisan.
polling_note: No public polling found for this race as of 2026-04-07.

candidates.name: Nicholas Bronni
candidates.summary: Nicholas Bronni is an associate justice of the Arkansas Supreme Court, appointed by Governor Sarah Huckabee Sanders and sworn in on January 1, 2025. Before joining the court, he served as Arkansas solicitor general and worked in the Arkansas Attorney General’s office, private practice, and federal service. His campaign describes him as an originalist who favors plain-language opinions and a limited judicial role.

summary_sources.title: Associate Justice Nicholas Bronni, Position 6 - Arkansas Judiciary

career_history.title: Associate Justice
career_history.description: Serves on Arkansas’s highest court after appointment by Governor Sarah Huckabee Sanders.

career_history.title: Solicitor General
career_history.description: Served as Arkansas Solicitor General, representing the state in major appellate and constitutional litigation.

career_history.title: Associate
career_history.description: Worked as an associate at Gibson Dunn & Crutcher LLP in Washington, D.C., before returning to Arkansas.

candidates.donor_summary: Arkansas Secretary of State campaign finance disclosures show Justice Nick Bronni had raised $213,725.00 as of his last filed report, with $114,245.00 from individuals, $70,150.00 from PACs, $18,300.00 from county political party committees, and $6,000.00 from political parties. The largest listed donors in the disclosure include multiple $3,500 contributions from Arkansas health-care and business PACs such as Arkansas Health Care Political Action Committee, Arkansas Health Care Senate Political Action Committee, Arkansas Pharmacists Political Action Committee, Businesses for Justice PAC, and AR People First, indicating a donor base concentrated in health care, business, and legal/political networks.
candidates.voting_summary: Nicholas Bronni is an incumbent Arkansas Supreme Court justice, not a legislator, so there is no legislative voting record to report. His public governing record is instead his judicial and solicitor-general work: the Arkansas Judiciary says he was appointed to the court in 2024 after serving as Solicitor General, where he argued cases including Delaware v. Pennsylvania and Rutledge v. PCMA, and his campaign site says he defended Arkansas laws on issues such as girls' sports, voter ID, and the state law barring contractors from boycotting Israel.

links.title: Associate Justice Nicholas Bronni, Position 6 - Arkansas Judiciary

Issue Spotlight: How Arkansas Supreme Court candidates want to improve state courts

Economy.stance: Bronni says he supports protecting taxpayers and tax cuts, and his campaign frames his record as opposing federal actions he says would harm Arkansas’s economy, including student loan debt cancellation. His campaign also says he has worked to block regulations that would hurt small businesses and family farms.

Guns & Safety.stance: Bronni’s campaign says he defended the Second Amendment against federal gun-control regulations. His campaign materials do not provide a broader public policy agenda on guns or public safety.

Foreign Policy.stance: Bronni has publicly supported Arkansas’s anti-boycott law and said he stood with Israel in defending it. His campaign and commentary focus on that issue rather than a broader foreign-policy platform.

sources.title: Anti-Israel boycotts are not OK in Arkansas

Election Reform.stance: Bronni’s campaign says he has defended Arkansas’s voter ID law and written briefs on election integrity. No broader election-reform platform was found in his public campaign materials.

Local Issues.stance: Bronni says he wants Arkansas courts to be clearer, more accessible, and more efficient, and his campaign emphasizes plain-English opinions and a limited judicial role. His public materials also highlight his work on state court cases and access to justice.

sources.title: Issue Spotlight: How Arkansas Supreme Court candidates want to improve state courts

Social Justice.stance: Bronni says he has worked on cases involving crime victims, retirees, free speech, religious liberty, and civil-rights issues in his roles as solicitor general and justice. No broader standalone social-justice platform was found in his campaign materials.

sources.title: Associate Justice Nicholas Bronni, Position 6 - Arkansas Judiciary

candidates.name: John Adams
candidates.summary: John Adams is an Arkansas attorney running for the state Supreme Court. His campaign says he has spent about 20 years representing Arkansans and emphasizes judicial independence, access to justice, and constitutional limits on government power.

summary_sources.title: About John | Support Fair Justice Today

career_history.title: Assistant Attorney General
career_history.description: Worked in the criminal division on criminal and constitutional appeals.

career_history.title: General Counsel
career_history.description: Served as legal counsel for an Arkansas community bank, with experience in banking regulation and business disputes.

career_history.title: Law professor
career_history.organization: University of Arkansas at Little Rock Bowen School of Law
career_history.description: Taught constitutional law and business law at Bowen Law School.

candidates.donor_summary: No current 2026 Arkansas Supreme Court campaign finance disclosure was found in the sources reviewed. The only finance record located for John Adams was a historical 2010 FEC filing for his congressional campaign, so it is not used here as current race funding information.
candidates.voting_summary: John Adams has not held legislative office, so there is no legislative voting record to report. His public governing record is instead his legal and advocacy work: his campaign says he has spent about 20 years representing Arkansans, including work on civil rights, defending librarians against book bans, and challenging court fees that burden low-income families; the Arkansas Supreme Court campaign site also describes him as a fifth-generation Arkansan with experience in banking and constitutional law.

links.title: Issue Spotlight: How Arkansas Supreme Court candidates want to improve state courts
How Arkansas Supreme Court candidates want to improve state courts
Adams the right justice for the Arkansas Supreme Court

Economy.stance: Adams says Arkansas's courts should be funded in a way that reduces the financial burden on individuals; he advocates reforming court fees and funding mechanisms to ensure the ability to pay is considered and to prevent debtors' prisons.

sources.title: Issue Spotlight: How Arkansas Supreme Court candidates want to improve state courts - Arkansas Times
Issue Spotlight: How Arkansas Supreme Court candidates want to improve state courts - Arkansas Advocate
Bronni vs. Adams for Supreme Court - Independent Arkansas

Social Justice.stance: Adams says he wants fair and impartial courts, equal treatment under the law, and broader access to justice in Arkansas. His campaign highlights pro bono work, protection of the right to read, and opposition to court practices that can trap low-income people in debt.

sources.title: About John | Support Fair Justice Today
Issue Spotlight: How Arkansas Supreme Court candidates want to improve state courts

Election Reform.stance: Adams supports nonpartisan judicial elections and presents himself as an independent voice focused on judicial restraint and constitutional limits on power.

flags.concern: The official Arkansas Judiciary URL for Nicholas Bronni appears to reference a different justice's position (Karen R. Baker, Position 6) rather than Bronni's own profile page. The URL path contains 'associate-justice-karen-r-baker-position-6' which suggests a possible copy-paste error or outdated reference. This same URL appears multiple times throughout Bronni's profile (summary_sources, voting_source_url, and links).
flags.suggestion: Verify and correct the official judiciary URL to point to Bronni's actual profile page on arcourts.gov. Contact Arkansas Judiciary directly or inspect their website structure to confirm the correct URL for Associate Justice Nicholas Bronni.

flags.concern: Nicholas Bronni is labeled 'incumbent: true,' but the profile's own description states that state law barred appointed justices from running for re-election 'to the same seat,' forcing two incumbent appointees to run for 'each other's seats.' The profile does not clarify whether Bronni is technically an incumbent justice (yes) or whether he is running for his own seat (no — he is running for Adams's seat). This creates ambiguity about what 'incumbent' means in this context.
flags.suggestion: Either: (1) add a clarifying note in the candidate summary or a new field explaining the seat-swap situation, or (2) revise the 'incumbent' label with additional context (e.g., 'incumbent justice, running for different seat'). This is important for readers to understand the race's unusual legal constraint.

flags.concern: The Gibson Dunn & Crutcher entry lacks start and end years ('start_year: null, end_year: null'), and the description refers to it as both 'international law firm' and a location in 'Washington, D.C.' without specifying his role duration or department. This is less precise than the other career entries.
flags.suggestion: If sources (e.g., campaign site, Federalist Society biography, LinkedIn) provide dates for his Gibson Dunn tenure, add them. If not, consider noting in the description that dates were not available in public sources.

flags.concern: Five of six listed sources for John Adams's Reproductive Rights stance are campaign page URLs (/issues, /issue, /policy, /policies, /priorities, /platform) that all appear to return 404 errors (as indicated by the agent's own notation in the Healthcare issue sources for Adams). These dead links suggest the agent was unable to access substantive campaign content on Adams's positions.
flags.suggestion: Remove 404 URLs from the sources list. If Adams's campaign site does not currently host a detailed issues page, update the source list to reflect only working URLs (e.g., his 'About' page or verified news coverage). Consider adding a note that Adams's detailed issue positions were not publicly accessible at the time of review.

flags.concern: Identical to the Reproductive Rights issue: multiple sources are campaign page URLs that return 404s (as flagged by the agent), making them unverifiable. This pattern suggests systematic difficulty accessing Adams's official campaign issue pages.
flags.suggestion: Consolidate and correct Adams's campaign sources. Remove 404-returning URLs and rely only on verifiable sources (campaign home page, social media, news interviews). If detailed issue pages are unavailable, note that Adams has not published a detailed public position on this topic.

flags.concern: The summary states Bronni was 'sworn in on January 1, 2025,' but does not specify the year of his appointment decision or confirmation. It also does not clarify the timeline of his appointment relative to the unusual race structure (elected justices must give up their seats, etc.). For readers unfamiliar with Arkansas law, the context is opaque.
flags.suggestion: Expand the summary or add a note clarifying: (a) the date of Bronni's appointment by Sanders, (b) the year he took office (2025), and (c) a brief explanation of the seat-swap rule that forced him to run for a different seat. This would help readers understand the race's unusual legal context.

flags.concern: The profile states 'No current 2026 Arkansas Supreme Court campaign finance disclosure was found' and only cites a 2010 FEC filing from Adams's congressional campaign (16 years old). Given the election date is March 3, 2026, and the profile was updated April 7, 2026, it is unclear whether Adams's 2026 campaign finance reports were available but not found, or whether they had not yet been filed/published. This leaves a significant gap in transparency for a sitting candidate.
flags.suggestion: Check Arkansas Secretary of State ethics disclosures (similar to the source cited for Bronni) for Adams's 2026 filings. If filings exist, retrieve and summarize them. If not yet public or filed, update the donor_summary to state: 'As of [date], 2026 campaign finance disclosures for Adams had not yet been published by the Arkansas Secretary of State.' This clarifies the reason for the gap.

flags.concern: John Adams's Local Issues stance is listed as 'No public position found' with no sources provided (no sources array, unlike Healthcare and Reproductive Rights). This inconsistency in documentation suggests the search may not have been completed or the result was not properly recorded.
flags.suggestion: Either add sources showing that the agent searched for local issue positions and found none, or ensure consistent structure across all issue entries (each should have a sources array or a note explaining why none is present).

reviews.summary: The profile is generally well-structured and demonstrates good-faith research effort, with multiple credible sources cited (official judiciary, campaign sites, news outlets). However, there are several concerning issues that require revision: (1) a significant URL mismatch in Nicholas Bronni's official judiciary profile (the cited URL references 'associate-justice-karen-r-baker-position-6' rather than Bronni's position), (2) inconsistency in how 'incumbent' status is labeled for Bronni given the race's unusual circumstances, (3) multiple campaign page URLs for John Adams that appear to return 404s, and (4) potential concern about whether Bronni's appointment date (January 1, 2025) aligns with his stated incumbency in a March 2026 election. The candidate backgrounds are generally credible and well-sourced, language is largely neutral, and absence of public positions on most issues is appropriately documented. The profile would benefit from URL verification and clearer explanation of the 'incumbent' designation given the race's complexity.

flags.concern: The source title listed is 'Associate Justice Nicholas Bronni, Position 6', but the URL contains 'associate-justice-karen-r-baker'. My training data suggests Karen Baker is the current occupant of Position 6. It is possible Bronni was appointed to this seat by 2026, but the mismatch between the URL name and the JSON title should be verified by a human reviewer to ensure the title wasn't hallucinated to match the candidate.
flags.suggestion: Verify if Nicholas Bronni was appointed to Position 6 prior to the 2026 election cycle.

flags.concern: The researcher correctly identifies that the only available FEC data for 'John Adams' is from a 2010 congressional run and excludes it from the 2026 totals. This is excellent attention to detail, but a human should confirm if 2026 state-level disclosures exist under a different filing name (e.g., 'John C. Adams').

reviews.summary: This is an exceptionally high-quality research profile. It provides granular campaign finance data (including specific PAC names and amounts), detailed professional histories for both candidates, and clear distinctions between their judicial philosophies. The agent correctly identified that as non-legislators, the candidates lack a 'voting record' and instead focused on their litigation and solicitor-general work. The sourcing is diverse, including official judiciary pages, ethics disclosures, and local news outlets like the Arkansas Advocate.

flags.concern: The URL provided appears to reference 'Associate Justice Karen R. Baker' instead of Nicholas Bronni, which may indicate a mismatch or typo in the source link, potentially affecting internal consistency.
flags.suggestion: Verify the URL directly to ensure it correctly pertains to Nicholas Bronni, and update if necessary for clarity.

reviews.summary: This profile is a strong, well-constructed overview of the 2026 Arkansas Supreme Court election, with thorough sourcing from official judiciary sites, campaign pages, and reputable news outlets like the Arkansas Advocate and Arkansas Times. It accurately documents candidate backgrounds, including career histories and education, while transparently noting absences like public polling or specific issue stances, demonstrating a balanced and diligent research effort.

validation_grade.summary: Validated by 2/3 reviewers with an average score of 86/100.
